title: AI regulation answers the wrong question
gist: David Nellor argues AI's central policy challenge is not regulation but institution-building, drawing on the Industrial Revolution's lag between technological and institutional innovation to argue governments should target the public goods and public bads AI creates rather than react to today's controversies.

insights.claim: The central AI policy challenge is not regulating the technology itself but building the institutions — rules, organisations, capabilities and coordinating mechanisms — that an AI-driven economy requires.
insights.speaker: David Nellor
insights.so_what: Reframes how to read AI policy flow: track institution-building (standards bodies, evaluation systems, capable regulators) as the real signal, not headline AI bills.
insights.locator: History suggests we should begin with a different question

insights.claim: AI, like electricity or the internet, is a general-purpose technology embedded across the economy, which makes it the wrong object of regulation in itself — the more useful question is what new economic and social functions it creates and what institutional capabilities those functions require.
insights.speaker: David Nellor
insights.so_what: Investors should watch for sector- and function-specific institutional responses (e.g. new standards bodies, evaluation markets) rather than expecting a single unifying AI regulatory framework.
insights.locator: Like electricity or the internet, AI is a general-purpose technology

insights.claim: Societies prosper not from regulating technological revolutions well, but from developing the institutions that let those revolutions create lasting economic, human, institutional and social capital.
insights.speaker: David Nellor
insights.so_what: A closing thesis-level claim: durable AI-driven growth will trace to institutional quality, not regulatory stringency — a factor to weigh when comparing jurisdictions' AI economic prospects.
insights.quote: Societies prosper not because they regulate technological revolutions particularly well – but because they develop the institutions that enable technological revolutions to create lasting economic, human, institutional and social capital.

insights.claim: In early industrialisation, Britain's government first focused on protecting existing economic interests via the Corn Laws, and only gradually shifted attention to institutions like factory inspectorates and public health authorities.
insights.speaker: David Nellor
insights.so_what: A historical template for how AI policy debates may evolve: initial protectionist reflexes give way to institution-building only after a lag.
insights.locator: go back to industrialisation and the transformation of Britain's economy

insights.claim: Britain's prosperity from industrialisation came not from regulating steam engines but from developing institutions — including evolved company law that mobilised capital for large-scale industrial enterprise — to address the collective-action problems industrialisation created.
insights.speaker: David Nellor
insights.so_what: Suggests capital-formation and corporate-law innovation, not technology-specific rules, will be the decisive institutional response enabling an AI economy.
insights.quote: Britain prospered not because it regulated steam engines, but because it developed institutions to address the collective action problems industrialisation created.
insights.locator: The Industrial Revolution again illustrates the point

insights.claim: Institutional lags after major technological change are inevitable because societies need time to discover which collective challenges the technology creates, but governments can shorten that lag by focusing on enduring governance needs rather than reactive policymaking.
insights.speaker: David Nellor
insights.so_what: Frames AI institutional lag as a design choice policymakers can compress — a variable to watch across jurisdictions moving faster vs slower on this.

insights.claim: A useful heuristic for whether a proposed AI institution is durable: if it would not still be needed 20 years from now, policymakers are probably responding to a transient technology rather than an enduring institutional requirement.
insights.speaker: David Nellor
insights.so_what: A concrete filter investors and policy-watchers can apply to separate durable AI-governance infrastructure plays from reactive, headline-driven rulemaking.
insights.quote: If it will not still be needed 20 years from now, policymakers are probably responding to a transient technology rather than an enduring institutional requirement.
insights.locator: The central question is therefore not this week's AI controversy

themes.theme: Public goods, public bads, and labour markets

insights.claim: Trusted evaluation systems, technical standards and governments capable of understanding AI are public goods unlikely to be adequately supplied by private incentives, while fraud, misinformation and cybercrime are public bads whose costs extend beyond those responsible for creating them.
insights.speaker: David Nellor
insights.so_what: Identifies specific under-provided public goods (AI evaluation/standards infrastructure) as a likely area of state investment or new market formation.
insights.locator: The implications become clearer when viewed through the lens of public goods and public bads

insights.claim: The relevant labour-market policy question is not whether AI changes jobs — since demand for labour has shifted with every major technological revolution — but whether AI creates new collective-action problems that existing labour-market institutions cannot address.
insights.speaker: David Nellor
insights.so_what: Pushes back on framing AI as a uniquely disruptive jobs threat; argues for strengthening education/training institutions over AI-specific labour interventions.
insights.locator: Concern over the consequence for labour offers another example

insights.claim: As an alternative to privacy regulation for AI-era personal data, governments could define property rights over personal identity and data that let individuals control, license or withhold their use through contracts, as Australia's data portability rights begin to do.
insights.speaker: David Nellor
insights.so_what: Flags a market-based alternative to data regulation — potential precedent for data-licensing markets as an investable institutional response to AI.
insights.locator: Current debates over personal data focus largely on privacy regulation

insights.claim: Regulation is only one institutional response to AI, and only a minority of AI-related issues are likely to require AI-specific regulation, since some challenges can be handled through existing institutions and legal frameworks.
insights.speaker: David Nellor
insights.so_what: Suggests AI-specific regulatory risk may be narrower than headline coverage implies, a useful calibration against over-pricing regulatory overhang.
insights.locator: Some challenges can be addressed through existing institutions
